nativeRange.habitat: Rocky reef substrates, coral rubble, and coarse sand in the shallow photic zone; frequently epiphytic on other algae and hard substrates

avoidWith: sea urchins (may graze heavily on calcareous algae)

imageCandidates.notes: Primary product image from Mosaic Macros listing for Thin Branching Coralline (Corallina sp.); shows lavender-purple thin articulated branches.
Secondary product image from consolidated Mosaic listing (Orange Encrusting Coralline variant, Corallina sp.).

summary: Thin Branching Coralline (Corallina sp.) is a heavily calcified red alga with slender, lavender-purple articulated branches resembling the growth form of SPS corals. It requires stable reef-level calcium and magnesium and performs best under high, full-spectrum lighting.
careNotes:
Thin Branching Coralline is a genus-level species within Corallina (family Corallinaceae, order Corallinales), closely related to Coral Weed ([sw-algae-031]) but distinguished by noticeably thinner, more delicate articulated branches with a lavender-purple coloration. Cuttings are typically sold at 1.5–2 inches on a plug or as 5-inch-plus frags; individual thalli generally reach 2–8 cm.

As a calcareous (geniculate) red alga — meaning its skeleton is built from calcium carbonate deposited between flexible joints — stable calcium and magnesium are essential for healthy growth. Target calcium around 400–450 ppm and magnesium around 1,250–1,350 ppm; alkalinity around 8–12 dKH is generally advised to support carbonate availability. Keeping nitrate in the 5–20 ppm range and phosphate at 0.02–0.1 ppm is recommended, as coralline algae tend to benefit from some measurable nutrients, though very high phosphate has been reported to inhibit calcification.

Lighting should be high intensity and full-spectrum; actinic-only spectrum is generally insufficient to support this species. Flow should be gentle to moderate, comparable to a soft-coral tank, with some surface agitation but not enough to tear the fragile branches.

This species must NOT be buried in sand — placing the base in substrate tends to cause melting at the attachment point. Secure fragments to live rock or coral rubble using wedging (preferred as the least stressful method), a loose zip-tie or fishing line, or superglue gel to a frag plug. Over time the holdfast will naturally attach to the rock surface.

Weekly dosing of an all-around micronutrient supplement containing iron and iodine is generally recommended for all macroalgae, including this species.

Growth rate is slow, making Thin Branching Coralline a manageable display specimen rather than a fast-growing refugium macro. It is generally considered reef-safe with standard invertebrates; sea urchins and aggressive herbivores have been known to graze calcareous algae heavily.
breedingNotes: Propagation in captivity is achieved primarily through fragmentation: detached thallus segments can attach to hard substrate and establish new growth. Preferred attachment methods are wedging into rock crevices, a loose zip-tie or fishing line, or superglue gel to a frag plug. Sexual reproduction through gametophyte-sporophyte alternation occurs in the wild but is not commonly observed in closed aquarium systems.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros — Thin Branching Coralline
primary.notes: Sole specialty hobby trade source for this species. Confirmed genus (Corallina sp.), growth form (thin lavender-purple articulated branches, SPS-like calcification), and size range (1.5–2" plug to 5"+ cuttings). Product page lacks explicit water parameter specifications; parameters sourced from Mosaic care guide.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros — Macroalgae Care: The Basics of Keeping Macroalgae in Your Reef
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source for all Mosaic-only entries. Provided canonical parameter targets: temp 22–26°C (72–78°F), pH 8.1–8.4, Ca 400–450 ppm, Mg 1,250–1,350 ppm, NO3 5–20 ppm, PO4 0.02–0.1 ppm. Also confirmed propagation methods (wedging, zip-tie/fishing line, superglue), red algae sand-bed melt warning, full-spectrum lighting preference, and weekly micronutrient dosing recommendation. Meets playbook 2-source minimum for Mosaic-only entries.
